title: Spaceflight supercharges anti-bacterial viruses
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 18 March 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we discover how a tick-derived protein could help to develop broad-spectrum anti-inflammatory therapeutics, watch jumping genes take over the biopharma pipeline and hear about ‘reverse vaccinology 3.0’.
Escherichia coli bacteria was flown to the International Space Station. (Cavallini James/BSIP/Universal Images Group/Getty Images (top); NASA (bottom))

Space supercharges anti-bacterial viruses

Viruses that infect bacteria, called phages, evolve different strategies to infect their targets on the International Space Station than they do on the ground, which could help create new treatments for antibiotic-resistant infections. Researchers found that the phages took longer to infect E.coli in microgravity, and that the viruses developed microgravity-specific mutations, some of which helped them to better cling onto bacterial receptors. Once they returned to earth, they were able to kill stubborn strains of E.coli responsible for urinary tract infections that tend to be resistant to bacteriophages.

Scientific American | 2 min read
 Reference: PLOS Biology paper

Tick protein lands one-two immune punch

Researchers have found a protein in tick saliva that blocks chemical signals called chemokines that drive inflammation in the body. When chemokines go haywire, they can cause inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and cancer. Proteins that block one type of chemokine have already been discovered in tick saliva, but immune conditions often involve two types — called CC and CXC — and this new protein blocks both. The novel shape of the double-action protein allows it to grip onto both types, offering a template for designing broader anti-inflammatory therapies.

Generic Engineering & Biotechnology News | 6 min read
 Reference: Structure paper

Anti-virals put the crosshairs on HIV

A new approach to HIV treatment that triggers infected cells to self-destruct, destroying the virus in the process, has shown promise in lab studies and some early trials. The treatment, which uses two existing anti-viral drugs, forces HIV to assemble an enzyme that the virus usually keeps broken into two halves. This enzyme triggers an internal alarm in cells where HIV persists — T cells and macrophages — which activates a cell-death pathway. The results were presented last month at the Conference on Retroviruses and Other Opportunistic Infections in Denver, Colorado.

Science | 6 min read

Approvals of the month

  • Single-dose sleeping sickness drug set for approval: Acoziborole (Acoziborole Winthrop) provides a one-off, oral drug for all stages of Gambiense human African trypanosomiasis. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)
  • FDA approves dwarfism drug: Navepegritide (Yuviwel) accelerates the growth of children aged 2 and up with achondroplasia, a common form of dwarfism. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)
  • GLP-1 drug approved for obesity in China: Ecnoglutide (Xianweiying) was also approved for the treatment of type 2 diabetes earlier this year. (Fierce Pharma | 3 min read)

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE

Agata Kieliszek and Jakob Magolan are two of the McMaster researchers behind Block Biosciences, which is working on a first-in-class precision medicine that will prevent secondary metastatic brain cancer. (Georgia Kirkos/McMaster University )

Start-up aims to stop cancer spread

A McMaster University start-up is developing a new treatment to prevent cancer from spreading to the brain, an innovation that could revolutionize the future of cancer care. The Block Biosciences team is working on a new, first-in-class precision medicine that would be taken proactively by patients after a diagnosis of lung, breast or skin cancer, to prevent secondary metastatic brain cancer, which happens when cancer cells from another part of the body travel to the brain.

Features and opinion

Jumping genes seize the biopharma pipeline

Most of the world’s biological medicines — such as insulin — are grown inside Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells: the workhorse of biopharma since the 1980s. But as drugs get more elaborate, genetically engineering CHO cells to reliably produce biomedicines is becoming a bottleneck. Even when using precise gene editing tools, such as CRISPR-Cas9, the DNA repair process needed to successfully insert a new gene only works in around 1 in every 100,000 cells. But transposons — or ‘jumping genes’ — are directly cut and pasted into genomes by transposase enzymes with relative ease. Newer versions of these enzymes can now stack multiple gene edits into one CHO cell.

Genetic Engineering & Biotechnology News | 10 min read

All good vaccinology comes in threes

In 2000, researchers debuted ‘reverse vaccinology’, a technique that aims to identify protective antigens in the genomic sequence of a pathogen for use in a vaccine. Then came ‘reverse vaccinology 2.0’, which uses antibodies isolated from people with infections to discover protective antigens and work out the structure of antigen-antibody complexes. Now, immunologists Emanuele Andreano and Rino Rappuoli and structural biologist Jason McLellan have described ‘reverse vaccinology 3.0’, which combines the previous two iterations with AI-enabled structural biology to accelerate the discovery of useful antigens from a years-long search to a matter of days.

Nature Reviews Microbiology | 9 min read

Faecal transplants boost immunotherapy

Three trials provide compelling evidence that faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) can boost the effectiveness of immunotherapy in advanced solid tumors, write oncologists Sumanta Pal and Diwakar Davar in an analysis of three studies published in Nature Medicine. But challenges for safety, donor selection and product development remain. Tumours partially or completely shrunk in 75% of FMT-treated people who underwent immunotherapy with melanoma, 80% of those with non-small cell lung cancer and 50% with metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC) across the studies. The only randomized, placebo-controlled trial, which evaluated immunotherapy plus an oral drug with or without FMT in individuals with metastatic RCC, did not find statistically significant 12-month progression-free survival. But it did find that FMT increased the proportion of people whose tumours shrunk (52% versus 32%).

Nature Medicine | 9 min read
 References: Nature Medicine paper 1, paper 2 & paper 3
Three studies suggest that FMT improves immunotherapy responsiveness not only by bolstering the growth of good bacteria, which improves the microbiome to help the immune system respond to cancer, but also by killing off bad bacteria. (Pal, S.K., & Davar, D./Nat. Med.)

Quote of the week

“We’ve crossed, I think, a threshold into what I’m calling the fourth generation of AI. Which is the knowledge-generating AI. We’ve been using it now basically to generate new ideas, and I feel like I’m consulting the oracle of Delphi.”

Mouse geneticist Gary Peltz is one of a small number of scientists in biomedicine harnessing machine learning. (Nature Medicine | 14 min read)

Is it ‘moral obligation’ or ‘tech bro’ hype: the eternal question. Scientists in the United States are attempting to stage a Jurassic Park-style revival of long forgotten species. After claiming to have revived the dire wolf, a controversial start-up is looking to reawaken the woolly mammoth and the dodo using ancient DNA and gene editing. But many scientists label the play arrogant hype that could erode already under-pressure endangered species protections.

Please send your vote on whether this falls under ‘moral obligation’ or ‘tech bro’ hype — I’m voting for the latter — and your feedback to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham and Jacob Smith
